summary: DHH announces the 1.0 release of Kamal, a deployment tool born from 37signals' cloud exit. He frames the project as a happy accident triggered by SUSE's exorbitant enterprise pricing, which pushed them to build their own open-source tooling. Kamal handles zero-downtime deploys and rolling restarts for both cloud VMs and bare metal. The tool has already been used to migrate seven production apps off the cloud, with projected savings of $7M over five years. DHH positions Kamal as useful not just for cloud exits but for any startup wanting to preserve the option of leaving the cloud later.
key_points: SUSE's overpriced enterprise contracts inadvertently motivated 37signals to build their own open-source deployment tooling
Kamal 1.0 offers zero-downtime deploys, rolling restarts, and asset bridging for both cloud VMs and bare metal
37signals has already migrated seven production apps including HEY, projecting $7M in savings over five years
The project crystallized a core value: critical infrastructure must be open source and simple enough for the whole team to understand
Kamal is positioned as valuable even for cloud-native startups who want optionality to leave later
money_quotes: So many of the best opportunities in life only reveal themselves when the easy path is blocked.
We will not build our critical infrastructure on top of anything but open source, and it has to be simple enough that our whole team can understand it. If such tools do not exist, we will build them.
We are creatures in search of the least resistance, so to reach higher, we usually need a little push.
I count my lucky stars that SUSE's pricing for Rancher and Harvester was so ridiculous over the top for our situation.
It's for anyone who wants the option of ever being able to easily do so.
These startups can still profit from using Kamal on day 1, so that they'll be able to leave, on their own accord, when it makes financial sense to do so.
key_insight: Enterprise vendor overreach can be a blessing in disguise, forcing teams to build simpler, open-source alternatives that deliver both independence and massive cost savings.
